title: Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with distributional drift and skew stochastic heat equation
overall_summary: The paper develops a solution theory for one-dimensional stochastic heat equations of the form ‚ąā_t u = 1/2 ‚ąā_{xx} u + b(u) + dot W when the drift b is a distribution in a Besov space B^ő≤_q, including finite measures and in particular b = őļőī_0. The main results are weak existence for ő≤ - 1/q > -3/2, strong existence and uniqueness in a natural drift-regularity class for ő≤ - 1/q ‚Č• -1 and ő≤ > -1, and unconditional strong well-posedness for finite non-negative measure drifts; the skew stochastic heat equation b = őļőī_0 is covered. The core technique is a family of stochastic sewing lemmas, combined with Besov heat-kernel regularization estimates, tightness of smooth approximations, a Gy√∂ngy-Krylov/Yamada-Watanabe argument, and a Davie-type critical uniqueness argument. For the original invariant-measure problem, the most useful contributions are the approximation theorem by smooth drifts, the comparison principle, the a priori C^{3/4}-type drift estimates for positive measure drifts, and the warning that the paper itself excludes Dirichlet boundary conditions because the uniform local nondeterminism estimate degenerates near the boundary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Assumption 2.1, Definitions 2.2-2.4: paper setting, distributional mild solution, and class V(őļ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix T0 > 0. The paper treats three heat-kernel settings: D = R with the free heat kernel g_t(x-y), D = [0,1] with periodic heat kernel p^{per}, and D = [0,1] with Neumann heat kernel p^{Neu}. The notation p denotes the corresponding kernel and P_t ŌÜ(x) = ‚ąę_D p_t(x,y) ŌÜ(y) dy is the heat semigroup. Let W be space-time white noise and V_t(x) = ‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy). For b in the Besov space B^ő≤_q(R), a sequence b_n of smooth bounded functions converges to b in B^{ő≤-}_q if sup_n ‚ą•b_n‚ą•_{B^ő≤_q} < ‚ąě and ‚ą•b_n - b‚ą•_{B^{ő≤'}_q} ‚Üí 0 for every ő≤' < ő≤. A measurable adapted process u on (0,T0] √ó D is a solution with bounded measurable initial condition u0 if there is a process K such that u_t(x) = P_t u0(x) + K_t(x) + V_t(x) a.s.; for every smooth bounded b_n converging to b in B^{ő≤-}_q, the quantities ‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y) b_n(u_r(y)) dy dr converge to K_t(x) uniformly in t ‚ąą [0,T0] and locally uniformly in x in probability; and u is a.s. continuous on (0,T0] √ó D. A solution belongs to V(őļ), őļ ‚ąą [0,1], if all pointwise moments are uniformly finite and, for every m ‚Č• 2, sup_{0<s‚Č§t‚Č§T0,x‚ąąD} ‚ą•u_t(x) - V_t(x) - P_{t-s}(u_s - V_s)(x)‚ą•_{L^m} / |t-s|^őļ < ‚ąě.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions rather than proved results. The point is that when b is an actual H√∂lder or bounded continuous function, choosing b_n converging uniformly recovers the usual mild formulation u_t = P_t u0 + ‚ąę_0^t P_{t-r} b(u_r) dr + V_t. When b is distributional, the nonlinear drift is defined only as the limit K of smooth drift convolutions. The class V(őļ) measures the time-regularity of this drift term after factoring out the stochastic convolution V and the heat flow.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate (2.5): uniform local nondeterminism of the stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's three settings D = R, D = [0,1] periodic, and D = [0,1] Neumann, the stochastic convolution V_t(x) = ‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy) satisfies, for every 0 ‚Č§ s ‚Č§ t ‚Č§ T0 and x ‚ąą D, the lower bound ‚ą•V_t(x) - E[V_t(x) | F_s]‚ą•_{L^2} ‚Č• ŌÄ^{-1/4} |t-s|^{1/4}. The estimate is uniform in x in these three settings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Conditioning on F_s leaves exactly the future-noise increment ‚ąę_s^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy). Its variance is ‚ąę_s^t ‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y)^2 dy dr = ‚ąę_s^t p_{2(t-r)}(x,x) dr. Lemma C.1 gives a uniform lower bound p_a(x,x) ‚Č• (2ŌÄa)^{-1/2}, and integrating a^{-1/2} over a ‚ąą (0,t-s) gives a constant times |t-s|^{1/2}; taking the square root yields the |t-s|^{1/4} bound. The paper explicitly notes that this uniformity fails for Dirichlet boundary conditions because the lower bound degenerates as x approaches the boundary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6: weak and strong well-posedness for Besov distributional drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (D,p) be one of the paper's three settings: R/free, [0,1]/periodic, or [0,1]/Neumann. Let ő≤ ‚ąą R, q ‚ąą [1,‚ąě], b ‚ąą B^ő≤_q(R), and u0 ‚ąą B(D), the bounded measurable functions on D. If ő≤ - 1/q > -3/2, then the stochastic heat equation ‚ąā_t u = 1/2 ‚ąā_{xx}u + b(u) + dot W has a weak solution in the sense of Definition 2.3, and this solution is in V(őļ) for every őļ ‚ąą (0, 1 + (ő≤‚ąß0)/4 - 1/(4q)] except őļ = 1. If ő≤ - 1/q ‚Č• -1 and ő≤ > -1, then in the class V(3/4) there is a unique strong solution.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is organized through the propositions in Section 3. First reduce to q ‚Č• 2 and ő≤ < 0 using Besov embeddings. For existence, approximate b by bounded continuous or smooth b_n and solve the classical mild equations. Proposition 3.2 gives uniform a priori bounds on the drift parts u^n - V in a conditional C^{1+ő≤/4-1/(4q)}-type seminorm. Lemma 5.4 and Proposition 3.3 give tightness of the drift-plus-noise fields in C_uc([0,T0]√óD). Proposition 3.4 identifies any limit as a solution to the distributional equation by decomposing K^{bar b_n;u} - K into a fixed-smooth continuity term, a Besov drift-approximation term, and a convergence term. Strong uniqueness in the critical range ő≤ - 1/q ‚Č• -1, ő≤ > -1 is Proposition 3.6: compare two solutions driven by the same noise, freeze the drift at the left endpoint, use critical stochastic sewing to control the residual, and close a Davie-type logarithmic Gr√∂nwall argument. Strong existence follows by applying the Gy√∂ngy-Krylov criterion to arbitrary pairs of approximation subsequences: any joint weak limit consists of two solutions driven by the same white noise, uniqueness forces the two limits to coincide, and hence the full approximation sequence converges in probability to a strong solution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.8 and Corollary 2.9: finite non-negative measure drifts and skew stochastic heat equation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let b be a finite non-negative Radon measure on R. In any of the paper's three heat-kernel settings and for every bounded initial condition u0, the stochastic heat equation with drift b has a unique strong solution in the sense of Definition 2.3. Consequently, the skew stochastic heat equation, namely the same equation with b = őļ őī_0 for őļ ‚ąą R, has a unique strong solution for every bounded measurable initial condition u0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 2.6 already gives existence and uniqueness in V(3/4) for measure drifts, since finite measures are covered by the Besov assumptions. The extra issue is to show that the auxiliary assumption u ‚ąą V(3/4) is automatic. This is Proposition 3.8. For non-negative b, choose smooth non-negative b_n approximating b. The approximating drift terms K^n_t(x) = ‚ąę_0^t ‚ąę p_{t-r}(x,y) b_n(u_r(y)) dy dr are non-negative and converge to K. Positivity implies the heat-flow monotonicity P_{T-t}K_t(x) ‚Č• P_{T-s}K_s(x), so őĽ^T_{s,t}(x) = P_{T-t}K_t(x) - P_{T-s}K_s(x) is a random control. Applying the stochastic sewing lemma with random controls to the frozen approximating drift yields K^n_t(x)-P_{t-s}K^n_s(x) ‚Č§ C(K_t(x)-P_{t-s}K_s(x))(t-s)^{1/2} + L^n_{s,t}(x), with ‚ą•L^n_{s,t}(x)‚ą•_{L^m} ‚Č§ C|t-s|^{3/4}. Passing to the limit and absorbing the first term on short intervals gives the V(3/4) estimate. The corollary for őļőī_0 is stated immediately after Theorem 2.8; őļ ‚Č• 0 is direct, and the paper records the conclusion for all real őļ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10: convergence and stability of smooth approximations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ő≤ ‚ąą R, q ‚ąą [1,‚ąě], ő≤ - 1/q ‚Č• -1, ő≤ > -1, b ‚ąą B^ő≤_q(R), and u0 ‚ąą B(D). Let b_n be bounded continuous functions converging to b in B^{ő≤-}_q, and let u^n_0 ‚ąą B(D) converge uniformly to u0. If u^n is the strong mild solution with drift b_n and initial condition u^n_0, then there exists a measurable u on [0,T0]√óD√óő© such that for every N > 0, sup_{t‚ąą(0,T0], x‚ąąD, |x|‚Č§N} |u^n_t(x)-u_t(x)| ‚Üí 0 in probability; u is a strong solution to the distributional equation with drift b and initial condition u0; and for every m ‚Č• 1, sup_{0<s<t‚Č§T0,x‚ąąD} ess sup_ŌČ E[|u_t(x)-V_t(x)-P_{t-s}(u_s-V_s)(x)|^m | F_s]^{1/m} / |t-s|^{3/4} < ‚ąě. In particular u ‚ąą V(3/4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the Gy√∂ngy-Krylov lemma. Take two arbitrary subsequences of the approximations. Proposition 3.3 gives tightness of the centered fields u^n - P_t u^n_0 together with V. After Skorokhod representation, Lemma B.2 reconstructs a white noise from the limiting stochastic convolution and ensures the two limiting fields are adapted to a common filtration. Proposition 3.4 identifies both limits as distributional solutions and provides the conditional regularity estimate. Proposition 3.6 then gives equality of the two limits. Since every pair of subsequences has a further joint subsequence whose two components coincide a.s., Gy√∂ngy-Krylov implies convergence in probability of the whole sequence. Uniform convergence of the deterministic initial heat flows supplies the convergence of u^n rather than just u^n - P_t u^n_0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.11: comparison principle for distributional drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ő≤ ‚ąą R, q ‚ąą [1,‚ąě], ő≤ - 1/q ‚Č• -1, and ő≤ > -1. Let b' and b'' be elements of B^ő≤_q(R), and let u'_0,u''_0 ‚ąą B(D). Let u' and u'' be the corresponding solutions. If u'_0(x) ‚Č§ u''_0(x) for almost every x ‚ąą D and b' ‚Čľ b'', meaning that b'' - b' is a non-negative Radon measure equivalently <b',ŌÜ> ‚Č§ <b'',ŌÜ> for every non-negative test function ŌÜ, then almost surely u'(t,x) ‚Č§ u''(t,x) for all t > 0 and x ‚ąą D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Mollify both drifts by the heat kernel on R: b'_n = G_{1/n}b' and b''_n = G_{1/n}b''. Heat convolution preserves the distributional order, so b'_n(x) ‚Č§ b''_n(x) pointwise. For smooth bounded drifts, the standard SPDE comparison principle gives u'^n(t,x) ‚Č§ u''^n(t,x) for all t,x. Lemma A.3 gives b'_n ‚Üí b' and b''_n ‚Üí b'' in B^{ő≤-}_q. Theorem 2.10 permits passage to the limit in probability, and continuity of the limits upgrades pointwise fixed-(t,x) inequalities to simultaneous inequalities over all t > 0 and x ‚ąą D.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2: uniform conditional a priori estimate for smooth or continuous approximations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the paper's standing reduction ő≤ < 0, q ‚ąą [2,‚ąě], and ő≤ - 1/q > -3/2, let f:R‚ÜíR be bounded continuous with f ‚ąą B^ő≤_q(R), let ő∑ ‚ąą B(D), and let u^{ő∑;f} solve the classical mild equation with initial condition ő∑ and drift f. For every m ‚ąą [2,‚ąě), there is C = C(ő≤,q,m,T0), independent of f and ő∑, such that [u^{ő∑;f}-V]_{C^{1+ő≤/4-1/(4q),0}L^m,‚ąě([0,T0])} ‚Č§ C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≤_q}. Here [Ōą]_{C^{ŌĄ,0}L^m,‚ąě} is the supremum over 0‚Č§s‚Č§t‚Č§T0 and x‚ąąD of ‚ą•‚ą•Ōą_t(x)-P_{t-s}Ōą_s(x)‚ą•_{L^m|F_s}‚ą•_{L^‚ąě} / |t-s|^ŌĄ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For bounded f the seminorm is initially finite with exponent at most one. Apply Lemma 5.2(i) to the drift increment Ōą_t-P_{t-s}Ōą_s = ‚ąę_s^t P_{t-r} f(V_r+Ōą_r) dr, taking ő≥ = ő≤ - 1/q, p = ‚ąě, and ŌĄ = 1 + ő≤/4 - 1/(4q), using the embedding B^ő≤_q into B^{ő≤-1/q}_‚ąě. This yields a local inequality of the form [Ōą]_{ŌĄ;[S,S+‚Ąď]} ‚Č§ C‚ą•f‚ą• + C‚ą•f‚ą•[Ōą]_{ŌĄ;[S,S+‚Ąď]} ‚Ąď^{3/4+ő≤/4-1/(4q)}. Since ő≤ - 1/q > -3/2, the exponent of ‚Ąď is positive, so choose ‚Ąď small enough to absorb the second term. To extend to [0,T0], subdivide any interval into pieces of length at most ‚Ąď and use the telescoping identity Ōą_t-P_{t-s}Ōą_s = ő£_k P_{t-s_k}(Ōą_{s_k}-P_{s_k-s_{k-1}}Ōą_{s_{k-1}}) together with heat-semigroup contraction in the conditional norms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.3-3.4 and Corollary 3.5: tightness, stability, and joint weak-limit construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the standing assumptions ő≤ < 0, q ‚ąą [2,‚ąě], ő≤ - 1/q > -3/2, let b'_n and b''_n be bounded continuous functions converging to b in B^{ő≤-}_q, and let u'_{0,n},u''_{0,n} converge uniformly to u0. If u'_n,u''_n solve the corresponding classical equations, and e u'_n = u'_n - P_t u'_{0,n}, e u''_n = u''_n - P_t u''_{0,n}, then Proposition 3.3 says that some subsequence of (e u'_n,e u''_n,V) converges weakly in [C_uc([0,T0]√óD)]^3. Proposition 3.4 says that if b_n‚Üíb in B^{ő≤-}_q, u^n_0‚Üíu0 uniformly, V^n has the same law as V, u^n solves the classical equation driven by V^n, and (e u^n,V^n) converges in probability in [C_uc]^2 to (e u,V), then u = e u + P_tu0 is a distributional solution and satisfies [u-V]_{C^{1+ő≤/4-1/(4q),0}L^m,‚ąě([0,T0])} ‚Č§ C sup_n ‚ą•b_n‚ą•_{B^ő≤_q}. Corollary 3.5 combines these facts: after a Skorokhod representation of any tight subsequential limit, there is a filtered probability space carrying a common white noise and two adapted weak solutions v',v'' of the limiting equation, with both satisfying the same conditional regularity bound.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For tightness, write u^n - P_tu^n_0 = K^n + V. Lemma 5.4 gives a Kolmogorov-type modulus for K^n: for every őī‚ąą(0,1), |K^n(t,x1)-K^n(s,x2)| ‚Č§ H_n(1+|x1|+|x2|)(|x1-x2|^őī+|t-s|^{őī/2}) with E H_n uniformly bounded in terms of the Besov norms. Arzela-Ascoli and Prokhorov give tightness. For stability, define K = e u - V and verify Definition 2.3. For a test approximation bar b_n, decompose |K^{bar b_n;u}-K| into: the difference between u and u^k for fixed smooth bar b_n, controlled by uniform convergence; the difference between bar b_n and b_k evaluated along u^k, controlled by Lemma 5.4 and convergence in B^{ő≤'}_q for ő≤'<ő≤; and K^{b_k;u^k}-K, controlled by convergence of (e u^k,V^k). Regularity passes to the limit by Fatou from Proposition 3.2. Corollary 3.5 uses Skorokhod representation, then Lemma B.2 to reconstruct a white noise from the limiting stochastic convolution and prove that the limiting processes are adapted to a common filtration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6: uniqueness criterion for two solutions driven by the same noise
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume ő≤ < 0, q ‚ąą [2,‚ąě], ő≤ - 1/q ‚Č• -1, and ő≤ > -1. Let u be a solution of the distributional SPDE with initial condition u0. Let v be a measurable process satisfying the representation and continuity parts of Definition 2.3 and the weaker approximation condition that there exists one smooth approximating sequence b_n‚Üíb in B^{ő≤-}_q for which ‚ąę_0^t P_{t-r} b_n(v_r) dr converges pointwise in probability to its drift term. Suppose u and v are adapted to the same filtration, both belong to V(3/4), and for some m ‚Č• 2, [u-V]_{C^{3/4,0}L^m,‚ąě([0,T0])} < ‚ąě. Then u = v almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set Ōą = u - V, ŌÜ = v - V, and z = u - v. For smooth approximations b_n define frozen increments H^{n,Ōą}_{s,t}(x) = ‚ąę_s^t ‚ąę p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}Ōą_s(y))dy dr, and similarly H^{n,ŌÜ}. Lemma 5.5 shows H^{n,Ōą} and H^{n,ŌÜ} converge in probability to H^Ōą and H^ŌÜ, with ‚ą•H^Ōą_{s,t}-H^ŌÜ_{s,t}‚ą•_{L^m} ‚Č§ C‚ą•z_s‚ą•_{BL^m}(t-s)^{1/2} and also ‚Č§ C(t-s)^{3/4}. Define the residual R_{s,t} = (Ōą_t-P_{t-s}Ōą_s-H^Ōą_{s,t}) - (ŌÜ_t-P_{t-s}ŌÜ_s-H^ŌÜ_{s,t}). Lemma 5.7, proved by the critical stochastic sewing lemma, gives for some őī>0 and every ŌĄ>1/2 the estimates ‚ą•z_t-P_{t-s}z_s‚ą•_{BL^m} and ‚ą•R_{s,t}‚ą•_{BL^m} bounded by C(‚ą•z‚ą•_{C^0L^m([s,t])}+‚ą•R‚ą•_{C^ŌĄL^m([s,t])})(t-s)^{1/2+őī} plus C‚ą•z‚ą•_{C^0L^m([s,t])}|log‚ą•z‚ą•_{C^0L^m([s,t])}|(t-s). On sufficiently short intervals the R term is absorbed, yielding a closed inequality for z. Continuity of t‚Ü¶‚ą•z_t‚ą•_{BL^m} follows from the equation and heat-continuity estimates. If z were nonzero, define decreasing hitting times t_k where ‚ą•z_{t_k}‚ą•_{BL^m}=2^{-k}; the inequality forces t_k-t_{k+1} ‚Č• c/(1+k), whose sum diverges, contradicting boundedness of the time interval. Hence z‚Č°0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8: positive measure drifts automatically satisfy V(3/4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If b is a finite non-negative Radon measure, then every solution of the distributional stochastic heat equation with drift b belongs to V(3/4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose smooth bounded non-negative b_n converging to b in B^{0-}_1 with uniformly bounded B^0_1 norm. For a solution u, define K^n_t(x)=‚ąę_0^t‚ąęp_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr; by the definition of solution, K^n‚ÜíK locally uniformly in probability along subsequences. Positivity implies the deterministic inequality P_{T-t}K^n_t(x) - P_{T-s}K^n_s(x) = ‚ąę_s^t‚ąęp_{T-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr ‚Č• 0, and after passing to the limit P_{T-t}K_t(x) ‚Č• P_{T-s}K_s(x). Thus őĽ^T_{s,t}(x)=P_{T-t}K_t(x)-P_{T-s}K_s(x) is a random control. Define frozen approximating increments A^{T,n}_{s,t}(x)=‚ąę_s^t‚ąęp_{T-r}(x,y)b_n(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}(u_s-V_s)(y))dy dr. The conditional mean of őīA is bounded by C|t-s|^{1/2}őĽ^T_{s,t}(x), while the martingale part has order |t-s|^{3/4}; these are exactly the hypotheses of Theorem 4.7. The resulting estimate, after setting T=t and passing n‚Üí‚ąě, is K_t-P_{t-s}K_s ‚Č§ C(K_t-P_{t-s}K_s)(t-s)^{1/2}+L_{s,t} with ‚ą•L_{s,t}‚ą•_{L^m}‚Č§C|t-s|^{3/4}. Absorb the first term on short intervals and patch intervals to obtain the V(3/4) seminorm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2: main regularization estimates for ‚ąęP f(V+Ōą)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:R‚ÜíR be bounded continuous and in B^ő≥_p(R). Let Ōą:[0,T]√óD√óő©‚ÜíR be measurable and adapted. Fix m‚ąą[2,‚ąě), n‚ąą[m,‚ąě], p‚ąą[n,‚ąě], ŌĄ‚ąą(0,1), and ő≥‚ąą(-2+1/p,0), with ő≥ - 1/p + 4ŌĄ > 1. Then there is C depending on ő≥,p,ŌĄ,m, and where relevant őī, but not on T, such that: (i) for every S‚ąą[0,T], sup_x ‚ą•‚ą•‚ąę_S^T‚ąę_D p_{T-r}(x,y)f(V_r(y)+Ōą_r(y))dy dr‚ą•_{L^m|F_S}‚ą•_{L^n} ‚Č§ C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}(T-S)^{1+ő≥/4-1/(4p)} + C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}[Ōą]_{C^{ŌĄ,0}L^m,n([S,T])}(T-S)^{3/4+ő≥/4-1/(4p)+ŌĄ}; (ii) for every őī‚ąą(0,1) and x1,x2‚ąąD, the L^m norm of the same integral with p_{T-r}(x1,y)-p_{T-r}(x2,y) in place of p_{T-r}(x,y) is at most C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}|x1-x2|^őī times (T-S)^{1-őī/2+(ő≥-1/p)/4} + [Ōą]_{C^{ŌĄ,0}L^m([S,T])}(T-S)^{3/4-őī/2+ő≥/4-1/(4p)+ŌĄ}; (iii) for every őī‚ąą(0,1) and bar T>0, the L^m norm of the integral with p_{T+bar T-r}(x,y)-p_{T-r}(x,y) is bounded by the same expression multiplied by bar T^{őī/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to Lemma 6.3, a stochastic-sewing estimate with a deterministic kernel X_r(y) satisfying ‚ąę_D |X_r(y)|dy ‚Č§ őď_X(T-r)^{-őĹ}. For smooth f, set A_{s,t}=‚ąę_s^t‚ąęX_r(y)f(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}Ōą_s(y))dy dr and A_t=‚ąę_S^t‚ąęX_r(y)f(V_r(y)+Ōą_r(y))dy dr. The increment őīA is controlled by the difference between Ōą_u and P_{u-s}Ōą_s. Conditional Gaussian estimates for V, in particular Lemma C.4, give factors (r-u)^{ő≥/4-1/(4p)}; Besov translation estimates give the dependence on the C^{ŌĄ,0} seminorm of Ōą. These verify the stochastic sewing hypotheses and yield the first estimate for general X. Taking X=p gives (i); taking X=p(x1,.)-p(x2,.) and using heat-kernel L^1 H√∂lder bounds gives (ii); taking X=p_{T+bar T-r}-p_{T-r} and the heat-kernel time-increment bound gives (iii). For bounded continuous f, approximate by heat mollifications G_{1/k}f and pass by dominated convergence while using uniform Besov bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 and Corollary 6.2: frozen-path Besov regularization and first/second difference estimates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 5.1 states that if f‚ąąB^ő≥_p is bounded, m‚ąą[2,‚ąě), p‚ąą[m,‚ąě], ő≥‚ąą(-2,0), 0‚Č§s‚Č§t‚Č§T, and őļ:D√óő©‚ÜíR is B(D)‚äóF_s-measurable with sup_x‚ą•őļ(x)‚ą•_{L^m}<‚ąě, then sup_x ‚ą•‚ąę_s^t‚ąę_D p_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}őļ(y))dy dr‚ą•_{L^m} ‚Č§ C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}(t-s)^{1+ő≥/4-1/(4p)}. Corollary 6.2 gives stronger forms. If X_r(y) satisfies ‚ąę|X_r(y)|dy ‚Č§ őď_X(T-r)^{-őĹ} with őĹ<1/2, the same frozen estimate holds conditionally with őď_X(T-s)^{-őĹ}. If őĽ‚ąą(0,1] and ő≥>-2+őĽ, then replacing őļ by two frozen fields őļ1,őļ2 gives a difference bound of order ‚ą•őļ1-őļ2‚ą•_{BL^m}^őĽ (t-s)^{1+(ő≥-őĽ)/4-1/(4p)}. If őĽ,őĽ1,őĽ2‚ąą(0,1], ő≥>-2+őĽ and ő≥>-2+őĽ1+őĽ2, a second-order difference involving őļ1,őļ2 frozen at F_s and őļ3,őļ4 frozen at F_u is bounded by a product term involving ‚ą•őļ1-őļ2‚ą•^{őĽ1}, a conditional ‚ą•őļ1-őļ3‚ą•^{őĽ2}, and a remainder involving ‚ą•őļ1-őļ2-őļ3+őļ4‚ą•^őĽ, with the corresponding time powers shown in formula (6.11).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 6.1 is the engine. For A_{s,t}=E_s‚ąę_s^t‚ąęX_r(y)h(V_r(y),r,y)dy dr, with h F_S-measurable in ŌČ and uniformly B^ő≥_p in the spatial argument, conditional Gaussian smoothing gives ‚ą•E_u h(V_r)‚ą• ‚Č§ C‚ą•h‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}(r-u)^{ő≥/4}(u-S)^{-1/(2p)}(r-S)^{1/(4p)}. This verifies the stochastic sewing increment estimates, and the Riemann sums converge by martingale orthogonality. To get the frozen estimates, take h(z,r,y)=f(z+P_{r-s}őļ(y)); Besov translation invariance gives the same norm as f. For first differences use ‚ą•f(¬∑+a)-f(¬∑+b)‚ą•_{B^{ő≥-őĽ}_p} ‚Č§ C|a-b|^őĽ‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}. For second differences use the Besov two-increment estimate ‚ą•f(¬∑+a1)-f(¬∑+a2)-f(¬∑+a3)+f(¬∑+a3+a2-a1)‚ą•_{B^{ő≥-őĽ1-őĽ2}_p} ‚Č§ C|a1-a2|^{őĽ1}|a1-a3|^{őĽ2}‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}, then apply heat-semigroup contraction to the random frozen fields.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1: stochastic sewing lemma with singularities and conditional norms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 0‚Č§S<T, m‚ąą[2,‚ąě), n‚ąą[m,‚ąě], and A_{s,t} be F_t-measurable for (s,t)‚ąąőĒ_{S,T}. Write őīA_{s,u,t}=A_{s,t}-A_{s,u}-A_{u,t}. Suppose that for u=(s+t)/2, ‚ą•E_s őīA_{s,u,t}‚ą•_{L^n} ‚Č§ őď1(u-S)^{-őĪ1}(T-u)^{-ő≤1}|t-s|^{1+őĶ1} with őĪ1,ő≤1‚ąą[0,1), őĶ1>0, and ‚ą•‚ą•őīA_{s,u,t}‚ą•_{L^m|F_S}‚ą•_{L^n} ‚Č§ őď2(u-S)^{-őĪ2}(T-u)^{-ő≤2}|t-s|^{1/2+őĶ2} with őĪ2,ő≤2‚ąą[0,1/2), őĶ2>0. Assume there is a process A_t such that Riemann sums ő£ A_{t_i,t_{i+1}} over partitions of [S,t] converge in probability to A_t. Then, with ő∑^{(őĪ,ő≤)}_{S,T}(s,t)=‚ąę_s^t(r-S)^{-őĪ}(T-r)^{-ő≤}dr, ‚ą•‚ą•A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}‚ą•_{L^m|F_S}‚ą•_{L^n} ‚Č§ Cőď2(ő∑^{(2őĪ2,2ő≤2)}_{S,T}(s,t))^{1/2}|t-s|^{őĶ2}+Cőď1ő∑^{(őĪ1,ő≤1)}_{S,T}(s,t)|t-s|^{őĶ1}, and ‚ą•E_S[A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}]‚ą•_{L^n} ‚Č§ Cőď1ő∑^{(őĪ1,ő≤1)}_{S,T}(s,t)|t-s|^{őĶ1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use dyadic partitions ŌÄ^k of [s,t]. Let A^k_{s,t}=ő£_i A_{t_i^k,t_{i+1}^k}. The dyadic refinement difference is A^{k+1}_{s,t}-A^k_{s,t}=ő£_i őīA_{t_i^k,u_i^k,t_{i+1}^k}. Decompose this into I^k, the sum of conditional expectations given F_{t_i^k}, and J^k, a martingale-difference sum. The I^k terms are bounded by summing the assumed conditional-mean estimate; the singular factors are integrated using elementary bounds like (B.6). The J^k terms are bounded by the conditional Burkholder-Davis-Gundy inequality and Minkowski, producing the square-root ő∑ term. Summing over k gives a Cauchy estimate between A^k and A^0=A_{s,t}. Passing k‚Üí‚ąě with the assumed Riemann-sum convergence and Fatou yields the conclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 4.5 and 4.7: critical stochastic sewing and stochastic sewing with random controls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 4.5 assumes the hypotheses of Theorem 4.1 with n=m and no singular factors őĪ_i=ő≤_i=0, and in addition assumes a borderline conditional mean estimate ‚ą•E_sőīA_{s,u,t}‚ą•_{L^m} ‚Č§ őď3|t-s| + őď4|t-s|^{1+őĶ4}. Then ‚ą•A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}‚ą•_{L^m} ‚Č§ Cőď3(1+|log(őď1T^{őĶ1}/őď3)|)(t-s)+Cőď2(t-s)^{1/2+őĶ2}+Cőď4(t-s)^{1+őĶ4}. Theorem 4.7 assumes a random control őĽ on őĒ_{S,T}, meaning őĽ(s,u)+őĽ(u,t)‚Č§őĽ(s,t) a.s., and the conditional mean bound |E_uőīA_{s,u,t}| ‚Č§ őď1|t-s|^{őĪ1}őĽ(s,t)^{ő≤1} with őĪ1+ő≤1>1, together with a martingale increment bound of the Theorem 4.1 type with no singularities. If Riemann sums converge to A_t, then there is a random error B_{s,t}, a functional of the midpoint increments, such that |A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}| ‚Č§ Cőď1|t-s|^{őĪ1}őĽ(s,t)^{ő≤1}+B_{s,t} a.s. and ‚ą•B_{s,t}‚ą•_{L^m} ‚Č§ Cőď2|t-s|^{1/2+őĶ2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Theorem 4.5, the dyadic decomposition from Theorem 4.1 gives two possible bounds on each refinement level: a summable supercritical bound involving őď1 2^{-kőĶ1}, and a critical bound őď3(t-s)+őď4 2^{-kőĶ4}(t-s)^{1+őĶ4}. Split the dyadic sum at level k. For levels below k use the critical őď3 estimate; for levels above k use the summable őď1 estimate. Optimizing k so that 2^{-kőĶ1}őď1T^{őĶ1} is comparable to őď3 yields the logarithmic factor. For Theorem 4.7, decompose dyadic refinements using conditional expectations at midpoints. The predictable part is bounded by őď1|t-s|^{őĪ1}ő£ őĽ(t_i,t_{i+1})^{ő≤1}; H√∂lder and superadditivity of őĽ give a summable factor 2^{-k(őĪ1+ő≤1-1)}őĽ(s,t)^{ő≤1}. The martingale differences are estimated by BDG and summed into B_{s,t}. Passing to the Riemann-sum limit gives the asserted pathwise estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas A.2-A.3 and measure/Besov embeddings used for approximation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma A.2 gives Besov translation estimates: for a tempered distribution f, ‚ą•f(a+¬∑)‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}=‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}; ‚ą•f(a1+¬∑)-f(a2+¬∑)‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p} ‚Č§ C|a1-a2|^őĪ‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^{ő≥+őĪ}_p}; and the analogous second-order translation difference is bounded by C|a1-a2|^{őĪ1}|a1-a3|^{őĪ2}‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^{ő≥+őĪ1+őĪ2}_p}. Lemma A.3 says that heat mollification G_t satisfies, for f‚ąąB^ő≥_p: if ő≥<0, ‚ą•G_t f‚ą•_{L^p} ‚Č§ C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}t^{ő≥/2}; G_t f‚Üíf in B^{bar ő≥}_p for every bar ő≥<ő≥; sup_t‚ą•G_t f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}‚Č§‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}; and if ő≥-1/p<0, ‚ą•G_t f‚ą•_{C^1}‚Č§C‚ą•f‚ą•_{B^ő≥_p}t^{(ő≥-1/p-1)/2}. The paper also uses the known embedding that finite signed measures belong to B^0_1, and Besov embeddings B^ő≤_{q1} into B^{ő≤-1/q1+1/q2}_{q2} for q1‚Č§q2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The translation estimates are proved dyadic block by dyadic block. Mean-value estimates and Bernstein inequalities give ‚ą•őĒ_j(f(¬∑+a1)-f(¬∑+a2))‚ą•_p ‚Č§ C|a1-a2|2^j‚ą•őĒ_jf‚ą•_p, while the trivial triangle inequality gives a zeroth-order bound; interpolation yields the fractional power. The second-order estimate is analogous, using two mean-value increments. The heat-mollification estimates use the Fourier support of dyadic blocks: for high frequencies, ‚ą•G_tőĒ_jf‚ą•_p‚Č§Ce^{-ct2^{2j}}‚ą•őĒ_jf‚ą•_p, and summing over j gives the t^{ő≥/2} smoothing. Convergence G_t f‚Üíf in lower Besov regularity follows from ‚ą•G_tőĒ_jf-őĒ_jf‚ą•_p‚Č§C(t2^{2j})^őĶ‚ą•őĒ_jf‚ą•_p. The C^1 estimate combines heat smoothing, the L^‚ąě Besov embedding, and standard heat-kernel derivative bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma B.2: reconstructing white noise from a limiting stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tilde V be a measurable random field on a filtered probability space with the same law as the stochastic convolution V. Then on the same space there exists a white noise tilde W such that tilde V_t(x)=‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_D p_{t-r}(x,y) tilde W(dr,dy). Moreover the natural filtrations agree: F^{tilde W}_t = F^{tilde V}_t. If an additional filtration tilde F_t contains F^{tilde V}_t and, for every s‚Č§t and test function ŌÜ, ‚ąę_D(tilde V_t(x)-P_{t-s}tilde V_s(x))ŌÜ(x)dx is independent of tilde F_s, then tilde W is a tilde F_t-white noise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define tilde W_t(ŌÜ)=<tilde V_t,ŌÜ> - 1/2‚ąę_0^t<tilde V_s,ŌÜ''>ds for test functions ŌÜ. Since tilde V has the law of V, these linear functionals have the finite-dimensional laws of white-noise integrals and extend by density to L^2(D). The distributional stochastic heat equation representation is equivalent to the mild stochastic convolution representation, giving tilde V as the convolution of tilde W. The equality of filtrations follows because tilde V is obtained by integrating tilde W forward, while tilde W_t(ŌÜ) is explicitly reconstructed from tilde V up to time t. For the enlarged filtration, approximate tilde W_t(ŌÜ)-tilde W_s(ŌÜ) by sums of independent heat-smoothed increments ‚ąę(tilde V_{s_i}-P_{s_i-s_{i-1}}tilde V_{s_{i-1}})Ōą_i and use the assumed independence plus L^2 convergence.

proof_techniques.name: Distributional drift defined by universal smooth approximation
proof_techniques.description: The paper avoids assigning a pointwise meaning to b(u) when b is a distribution. Instead it defines the drift term K as the limit, in probability and locally uniformly in space-time, of the classical mild drift convolutions obtained from every smooth bounded approximating sequence b_n‚Üíb in B^{ő≤-}_q. A key part of the theory is showing that, in the well-posedness regimes, this limit is independent of the approximating sequence and coincides with the limit of constructable smooth solutions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already assumes this solution notion and approximation by G_{1/n}. This framework suggests proving semigroup or invariant-measure facts first for smooth Gaussian drifts G_{1/n} and then passing to the őī_0 limit using Theorem 2.10-type convergence.

proof_techniques.name: Stochastic sewing as regularization by space-time white noise
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly estimates time integrals of singular functions of V+Ōą by treating left-endpoint frozen approximations as a two-parameter process A_{s,t}. Stochastic sewing converts conditional Gaussian smoothing and martingale increment bounds into estimates for the full nonlinear integral. This yields powers such as 1+ő≥/4-1/(4p), reflecting the Hurst-1/4 behavior of the stochastic heat equation at fixed spatial points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any attempt to prove continuity of transition probabilities, tightness of stationary laws, or stability under approximation for the Dirichlet őī_0 equation can try to reuse these estimates, assuming the Dirichlet analogue of the local nondeterminism input is available.

proof_techniques.name: Smooth approximation plus Gy√∂ngy-Krylov/Yamada-Watanabe upgrade
proof_techniques.description: The existence proof first solves equations with bounded continuous drifts, proves tightness of the centered fields, identifies all weak limits as distributional solutions, and then uses pathwise uniqueness to force all subsequential limits to agree. The Gy√∂ngy-Krylov criterion then gives convergence in probability of the entire approximation sequence, producing a strong solution.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For invariant measures, one can try to analyze invariant or long-time laws for the smooth approximating semigroups and use tightness plus uniqueness of subsequential limits to identify any limiting stationary law of the singular equation.

proof_techniques.name: Comparison by mollification of ordered distributions
proof_techniques.description: The comparison principle is obtained by mollifying ordered Besov distributions b'‚Čľb'', using the classical comparison theorem for smooth reaction terms, and passing to the limit with the approximation theorem. The order b'‚Čľb'' is exactly distributional positivity of b''-b'.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since őī_0 is a non-negative measure, this gives monotone comparisons with equations having smaller or larger ordered drifts, which may support coupling, domination, or tightness arguments for invariant measures.

proof_techniques.name: Positive-measure random control for automatic drift regularity
proof_techniques.description: For non-negative measure drifts, positivity of the approximating drift makes P_{T-t}K_t - P_{T-s}K_s non-negative and superadditive in time. The paper treats this quantity as a random control in a stochastic sewing lemma, obtaining the V(3/4) drift regularity for every solution without assuming it a priori.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original drift őī_0 is positive. This mechanism is likely the most relevant a priori estimate for stationary or invariant-law arguments, because it gives uniform control of the singular drift term in the same regularity class used for well-posedness.

proof_techniques.name: Critical Davie-Gr√∂nwall uniqueness with logarithmic loss
proof_techniques.description: At the critical Besov index ő≤-1/q=-1, the sewing estimate produces a borderline term of size ‚ą•z‚ą• |log‚ą•z‚ą•| (t-s). The paper closes uniqueness by considering hitting times at levels 2^{-k}; the logarithmic term forces time gaps of order 1/k, whose divergent sum contradicts finite time if the difference ever becomes positive.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one attempts a coupling proof showing that two stationary solutions driven by the same noise must coincide, this hitting-level/logarithmic Gr√∂nwall argument is a ready-made pathwise uniqueness mechanism.

proof_techniques.name: Weak-limit preservation of the driving white noise
proof_techniques.description: When applying Skorokhod representation to stochastic convolutions, the paper reconstructs the white noise from the limiting convolution using the distributional heat equation. Independence of future increments is checked through heat-smoothed increments of the limiting V.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If stationary approximations are taken to a weak limit, this method can identify the limit as a genuine weak solution driven by white noise rather than just as an abstract random field.

proof_techniques.name: Besov heat mollification calculus
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically uses heat mollification G_t b to approximate distributions in lower Besov regularity while keeping the B^ő≤_q norm bounded, and uses Besov translation inequalities to quantify how f(V+a)-f(V+b) behaves when f is distributional. These estimates are the analytic backbone of the stochastic sewing bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original approximants G_{1/n}őī_0 are exactly of this form. The uniform Besov norm control and convergence in B^{ő≤-}_q are the inputs needed to justify passing from smooth SPDEs to the őī_0 SPDE.

proof_techniques.name: Awareness of boundary dependence through local nondeterminism
proof_techniques.description: The authors deliberately avoid Dirichlet boundary conditions because their proof uses a lower bound on the conditional variance of V_t(x) uniformly in x, and the corresponding Dirichlet stochastic convolution has variance degenerating near the boundary. They state that the Dirichlet case should be adaptable but would introduce additional technical difficulties.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem assumes the needed Dirichlet extension. Any proof porting paper estimates should explicitly check which uses require uniform-in-x bounds and whether boundary localization or weighted estimates are needed.

other_useful_info: The paper itself proves no invariant-measure, ergodicity, strong Feller, or irreducibility theorem. It cites Bounebache-Zambotti [BZ14] for a Dirichlet-form construction of weak solutions to the skew stochastic heat equation, Gy√∂ngy-Pardoux [GP93a, GP93b] for comparison and regularization of SPDEs with rough but functional drifts, and Butkovsky-Mytnik [BM19] for path-by-path uniqueness of stochastic heat equations with bounded drift. Theorem 2.12, not central to invariant measures, shows that certain rescaled smooth reaction-diffusion SPDEs converge to SPDEs with homogeneous distributional drifts; in the absolutely integrable case this gives a scaling limit to the skew stochastic heat equation with őī_0 coefficient equal to the integral of the reaction term. The paper notes that finite signed measures lie in B^0_1 and hence are included in the Besov framework via embeddings; this is the basic reason őī_0 is in the critical well-posedness class.
why_relevant: This is the core paper defining and proving well-posedness/approximation for stochastic heat equations with distributional drift, including b = őī0. Its approximation framework is directly needed for transferring irreducibility, Feller, or invariant-measure arguments from smooth drifts to the singular limit.
